# Questions in Livestream 204

Live Stream #204: Never Ending Popcorn

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:40</a> | Will David release a comic book or graphic novel? | Possibly, but not soon because current project commitments are already booked across fiction and scholarship work. | He says it is possible, but his publishing rhythm is already planned: cultural-engagement projects in the first half of the year and scholarship projects in the second half. He points to fiction such as Requoy of the Dead and its sequel as current soft-entry archaeology projects, while later slots are reserved for nonfiction such as Exodus or chronology work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:20</a> | Do George and Kogan themselves say Hebrew descends from Amorite, or is that an outside inference? | He said that is mainly an external inference drawn from their comparative data, not the exact claim they make. | He says George and Kogan compare Amorite tablets with Hebrew, Arabic, Aramaic, and Akkadian rather than directly narrating Hebrew's descent from Amorite. In his reading, the many close Hebrew-Amorite comparisons and the older date of the Amorite material make the inference natural, even if the authors leave the more controversial biblical-studies conclusion unstated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:16</a> | Do you think Brian Windle's views on Jericho could become the new ABR position? | He thought it might take a generation or two, but not soon, because ABR remains heavily invested in the 1446 BC date. | He explains Windle as more open to a Septuagint-based chronology and to Nigro's Late Bronze destruction evidence, but he thinks ABR still repeatedly returns to the Masoretic 480 years and the 1446 BC Exodus date. Because that older position is institutionally entrenched, he expects any shift to be slow and perhaps dependent on a generational turnover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=1521" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:21</a> | Is the Eucharist symbolic or the real presence? | He said that framing is the wrong either-or question; the deeper debate concerns authority, priesthood, and apostolic succession. | He says the Eucharist is both symbolic and, in some sense, connected with real presence, so the binary framing obscures the real disagreement. For him, the underlying debate is whether an ordained priesthood in apostolic succession is required to invoke the sacramental act, making the issue one of authority and ecclesial office more than simple symbolism versus presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=1903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:43</a> | What is the significance of Moses and Elijah appearing at the Transfiguration? | He said the scene shows continuity, with Moses and Elijah affirming Christ and his divinity. | He says the point is continuity: Elijah represents the prophetic expectation of return, while Moses is the foundational prophetic figure. Their appearance at the Transfiguration functions as approval of Christ, with two of Israel's greatest figures affirming him as he reveals his divinity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=2107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:07</a> | Should Numbers 36:13 be in Deuteronomy 1? | He said yes; he views it as better fitting the opening of Deuteronomy and as likely misplaced in the Torah scroll tradition. | He argues that Numbers 36 is discussing clan and inheritance matters, while Numbers 36:13 sounds like the setup for Deuteronomy's legal discourse. Because Torah scrolls are not divided into separate books in the same way, he sees it as plausible that a verse marking the transition into Deuteronomy was positioned at the end of Numbers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=2294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:14</a> | How does marriage in the ancient Near East compare with marriage in Israel and later Christianity? | He said much of the wider ancient Near East treated marriage more as a private contract, while Israel and Christianity give it stronger religious meaning. | He contrasts Israelite and Christian ideas of God joining spouses with much of the ancient Near East, where marriage was treated primarily as a contract between families or individuals. In places such as Egypt, he says priests did not sanctify marriages, so divorce and remarriage belonged more to private agreement than to a religious institution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=2511" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:51</a> | Did Jeremiah write Kings? | Probably not. He said Kings looks like an exilic or later compilation from earlier sources. | He rejects a simple Jeremiah authorship model because Kings appears to be stitched together from sources and edited from an exilic perspective. Jeremiah could be one possible source or influence, but the final book looks like a later compilation rather than a single prophetic composition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=2656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:16</a> | Could the last part of Kings be an addition by somebody else? | He doubted it, arguing that Kings as a whole shows signs of compilation from sources rather than a simple late add-on. | He says the issue is not best explained by imagining only the ending as a later supplement. Since Kings already bears signs of source use and editorial assembly, the closing material fits the broader compositional pattern instead of standing out as a lone attachment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=2728" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:28</a> | Did Jeremiah write Chronicles? | He said probably not, since Chronicles also looks like a later compilation and refers to the Persian period. | He says Chronicles is even less likely to be by Jeremiah because its perspective and references fit a later period, including Persian-era concerns. Like Kings, it appears to be compiled from earlier records rather than authored directly by Jeremiah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=2835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:15</a> | Why wait until the high priest is dead in Numbers 35:25? | He said the manslayer remains in exile until the high priest's death satisfies the blood-guilt issue, after which he can return. | He explains the city of refuge as protection for manslaughter, not murder, where accidental bloodshed still leaves blood guilt unresolved. The manslayer's exile lasts until the high priest's death supplies the life-for-life satisfaction, after which the person can return safely to his land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=3143" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:23</a> | Is Mount Hor in Numbers 33:7 Mount Horeb? | No. After checking the passage, he corrected himself and said Mount Hor is near Edom and Kadesh, not Horeb/Sinai. | He initially thought Hor might be another name for Horeb/Sinai, then read the Numbers context and corrected himself. Since the passage places Mount Hor near Edom and Kadesh and associates it with Aaron's death, he concludes it cannot be Mount Horeb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=3278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:38</a> | Did cities of refuge apply only to manslaughter or also to other accidental crimes? | He said only manslaughter, because other accidental wrongs could be resolved by fines, while a murder charge could not. | He says cities of refuge exist because accidental killing creates a unique problem: a blood avenger may treat the case as murder, and murder cannot simply be paid off with a fine. Other accidental damages can be financially compensated, so they do not require refuge exile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=3344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:44</a> | How large were the cities of refuge? | He said there is no general consensus; presumably they were large enough to mount a defense, but exact sizes are unknown. | He says the transcript does not support a specific size estimate. His cautious inference is that they had to be defensible enough to protect the manslayer from an avenger, but he stresses that the exact dimensions are not known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=3406" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:46</a> | Where can I get a map of the Numbers 33 route? | He recommended Barry Beitzel's Moody Atlas of the Bible, while stressing the route becomes uncertain after Rephidim. | He recommends Barry Beitzel's Moody Atlas of the Bible, preferably the second edition or later, because it gives a useful Exodus route map. He still cautions that the route becomes highly uncertain after Rephidim, so any map should be treated as a reconstruction rather than a settled itinerary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=3469" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:49</a> | Why did only half of Manasseh stay across the Jordan? | He said they wanted a desirable inheritance immediately instead of waiting for Shiloh, and that choice had later consequences. | He describes the decision as impatience and instant gratification: they saw land east of the Jordan that looked good and took it instead of waiting for the later allotment at Shiloh. He sees later separation from the main body of Israel as one consequence of choosing that inheritance outside Canaan proper. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=3858" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:18</a> | Can anyone leave a review on Goodreads? | Yes. He said anyone can leave a Goodreads review with an account, and reviews matter strongly for book visibility. | He says Goodreads reviews are open to ordinary account holders and are not limited to professional reviewers. He stresses that reviews help books sell because readers often notice the cover first and then use reviews as a major trust signal before buying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:57</a> | Why does the Bible often refer to Manasseh as the son of Joseph but not Ephraim the same way? | He said it highlights Joseph's double portion through Ephraim and Manasseh; Ephraim received the blessing, but Manasseh still belonged to Joseph's inheritance. | He ties the phrasing to Joseph's doubled inheritance through his two sons. Ephraim becomes dominant because of the blessing, but Manasseh is still explicitly marked as Joseph's son to preserve the inheritance logic and show how Joseph's portion is represented in the tribal structure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4311" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:51</a> | What evidence is there that the two-powers model of the Godhead dates back to the monarchial period? | He pointed to Alan Segal's work and said Second Temple sources preserve a theology with roots in earlier biblical texts. | He points to Alan Segal as the major scholarly reference for the two-powers discussion, while arguing that Second Temple texts did not invent the idea from nothing. In his view, those later sources preserve and develop patterns already present in older biblical material from Israel's monarchic traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4514" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:14</a> | Was Numbers 32:22 fulfilled by the tribes, since Judah and Dan never completely conquered their areas? | He said the cited verse may not fit the question exactly, but Judah eventually secured its inheritance under David while Dan abandoned its inheritance. | He distinguishes the verse from the larger conquest problem and says it may not answer the question as framed. Judah's territory eventually becomes secure under David, while Dan is the clearer failure case because it abandons its allotted inheritance and relocates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:33</a> | Did you lose weight or gain weight in Egypt? | He said he lost about five pounds on the most recent trip, which he considered relatively good for travel in Egypt. | He answers from personal experience rather than making a broader claim about travel. On the most recent Egypt trip he lost about five pounds, which he regarded as a good outcome given the disruption and food patterns of travel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:27</a> | Did you ever have pigeon when you went to Egypt? | Yes, on a previous trip, but he said he did not like it because it was tough, dry, and rubbery. | He says he tried pigeon on an earlier Egypt trip but did not enjoy it. His complaint was texture and preparation: it came across as tough, dry, and rubbery rather than appealing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:55</a> | Does camel taste like goat? | No. He said camel is closer to a sweet, tender beef than goat. | He distinguishes camel from goat and says it tastes much closer to beef. In his description, the camel he had was sweet and tender rather than having the flavor profile he associates with goat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=4980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:00</a> | Does donkey meat taste like horse meat? | He said he would imagine so, but he has not tried donkey meat. | He gives only a speculative answer because he has not personally eaten donkey. The comparison to horse meat is presented as a guess, not as firsthand knowledge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=5071" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:31</a> | Are you good at wine tasting? | He said yes, describing himself as having a discerning palate and a preference for tannin-rich Bordeaux-style wines. | He says he has a good palate and can distinguish wine characteristics, especially tannins. He favors Bordeaux-style wines and uses that preference to explain the kinds of flavors and structures he notices when tasting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=5322" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:42</a> | Is it weird if I want to make my own denomination? | He said many people think about that at some point, but implied the practical burden is substantial. | He treats the impulse as understandable rather than uniquely strange, but he frames it as far more serious than simply preferring one's own theology. The implied caution is that building a denomination brings administrative, pastoral, doctrinal, and institutional responsibilities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=5417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:17</a> | What should I know about trying to make wine from pineapple? | He said pineapple is difficult because its acidity can kill yeast, so acidity has to be buffered or controlled. | He says pineapple can be fermented, but its acidity makes it difficult because yeast may be inhibited or killed. The practical issue is managing pH or buffering acidity so fermentation can proceed rather than assuming pineapple behaves like grapes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=5770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:10</a> | Was alcohol used as currency in post-World War I Germany or post-World War II Hungary? | He said yes, comparing it to soldiers trading vodka for food and noting alcohol can function as currency when money collapses. | He says alcohol can become a barter good when official currency collapses or becomes unreliable. He compares it to soldiers using vodka to trade for food, because alcohol is portable, desired, and recognizable in crisis economies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=5865" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:45</a> | What happens if you break a vow in Numbers 30:15? | He said the husband bears responsibility if he tries to annul a vow after the allowed time; he effectively owns the obligation. | He reads the passage as giving the husband a limited window to annul a vow after hearing it. If he waits too long and then tries to overturn it, the responsibility transfers to him, so he bears the consequences of the obligation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=6103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:43</a> | If the husband annuls the vow after hearing it but still pays it, did he really annul it? | He said he tries to annul it, but the court would still hold him responsible for the obligation. | He distinguishes intention from legal effect: the husband may attempt to annul the vow, but if the permitted period has passed, the obligation remains enforceable. Paying it afterward shows that the court or community would still treat him as liable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=6347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:47</a> | Is legal moonshine an oxymoron? | He said no; moonshining is illegal production, but moonshine as white whiskey can be sold legally in some places. | He separates the historical activity of moonshining from the commercial product labeled moonshine. Illegal distillation is moonshining in the strict sense, but legally produced white whiskey can be marketed as moonshine without contradiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=6442" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:22</a> | Is pasteurization legally required? | He said it depends on the country; it is required in places such as Canada, the United States, and England, but not everywhere. | He gives a jurisdiction-dependent answer rather than a universal rule. In his examples, Canada, the United States, and England require pasteurization in relevant contexts, while other places allow more unpasteurized dairy access. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=6662" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:02</a> | Shouldn't imported European cheese be very expensive in Canada? | He said it can be, but enough bulk shipping can defray the cost. | He says imported cheese may be expensive, but transportation cost is not always decisive when products are shipped in sufficient volume. Bulk importation spreads shipping expense across many units and can keep some European cheeses available at tolerable prices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=6688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:28</a> | Would you rather try buying moonshine or raw milk? | He said neither; store-bought moonshine makes illegal buying unnecessary, and raw milk is too risky and hard to get locally. | He rejects both options in practice. Legal store-bought moonshine removes the need to seek illegal alcohol, while raw milk poses health and availability concerns that make it unattractive to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=6730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:10</a> | What are your thoughts on the archaeological discovery at the Church of the Holy Sepulchre? | He said first-century grape and olive pollen and pottery support the site's being a garden and an early venerated location. | He says the reported finds matter because grape and olive pollen fit the Gospel description of a garden setting, while pottery and associated material point to an early context. He treats this as part of a cumulative case that the Holy Sepulchre site was remembered and venerated very early. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:38</a> | Didn't they find first-century pollen on the Shroud of Turin? | He said the pollen claim is only partly useful because some species fit the broader region, but the samples could not be securely dated. | He says pollen evidence on the Shroud is limited because some identified species are not specific enough to prove a precise location. More importantly, the pollen itself cannot be securely dated to the first century, so it cannot do the same kind of work as dated archaeological context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7206" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:06</a> | Does the Holy Sepulchre discovery mean Jesus was venerated since the first century and always seen as God? | He said it supports a growing case for early veneration of Jesus, though he treated it as evidence rather than standalone proof. | He says the discovery strengthens the argument for early reverence at the site, but he does not treat one discovery as proving the whole theological claim by itself. It contributes to a broader evidentiary picture for early veneration rather than independently settling Christology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:05</a> | If the site was esteemed since the first century, would that be evidence for its legitimacy? | Yes, as evidence but not proof; he said the garden context strengthens the case for the Holy Sepulchre location. | He distinguishes evidence from proof: early esteem of the site would make the traditional identification more plausible, especially if the archaeology supports a garden context. But he still frames it as one line of support within a cumulative case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:28</a> | Do you miss babysitting us kids all day? | He joked that he misses keeping the audience out of trouble. | He answers jokingly rather than as a substantive topic, saying he misses making sure the audience behaves and stays out of trouble. The exchange functions as light livestream banter between heavier questions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:49</a> | Why is there no sea in heaven? | He said the sea symbolizes death, chaos, danger, and uncertainty in Israelite thought, not necessarily the absence of all water. | He explains the sea as an ancient Israelite symbol of chaos, danger, death, and unstable forces opposed to ordered life. Therefore Revelation's no sea language should not be reduced to a claim that there will be no water, beaches, or aquatic beauty in the new creation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:18</a> | What happened to the body of an average Egyptian foot soldier who died on campaign, and how did families find out? | He said the soldier would usually be buried on the way, and families found out when the army returned and the scribe's records were consulted. | He says an ordinary soldier who died on campaign would likely receive a practical burial along the route or near the battlefield rather than being brought home. The family would learn later through returning army personnel and scribal records, since the army tracked who was present and who did not return. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=7894" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:34</a> | Are iron chariots in Joshua anachronistic for the Bronze Age? | No. He said the phrase refers to iron fittings or axle assemblies, not all-iron chariots, and iron was available by that transition period. | He says the phrase should not be imagined as a fully iron vehicle. It likely refers to iron-reinforced parts such as fittings, tires, or axle components, and the relevant late Bronze to early Iron transition makes that kind of iron use plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=8409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:09</a> | Why didn't the Philistines become seafaring traders like the Venetians if food was scarce? | He said they lacked deep-sea ports and had little to trade, unlike peoples with better port access. | He says geography and economy limited them. Unlike a maritime trading power with deep harbors and valuable export goods, the Philistines did not have the same port infrastructure or trade base, so food pressure did not automatically make them a Venetian-style seafaring state. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=8725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:25</a> | Why were the port and Pi-Ramesses both abandoned? | He said the Pelusiac branch silted up, making ship access to the deep harbor unsustainable despite dredging attempts. | He says the abandonment was driven by environmental and logistical change: the Pelusiac branch silted up, cutting off effective access to the harbor system. Once dredging and maintenance could no longer keep it usable, both the port function and the city dependent on it declined. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=8782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:22</a> | If the Bible prohibits magic, how could people know what magic is before they knew what science is? | He said magic is defined in relation to religion and ritual manipulation of nature, not as the opposite of science. | He says the question assumes a modern science-versus-magic contrast that is not how ancient people defined the category. Magic was understood as ritual action aimed at manipulating natural or spiritual forces, so people could identify it without needing a modern concept of science. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=8862" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:42</a> | What is the definition of magic? | He defined magic as ritual appeal to or manipulation of natural forces, distinguishing disruptive and sympathetic magic. | He defines magic as ritualized appeal to or manipulation of natural forces outside proper worship. He distinguishes disruptive magic, which tries to interfere with reality or other persons, from sympathetic magic, which uses symbolic correspondence to produce an effect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=9135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:15</a> | Could you say the health system has some magic in it since it works against nature? | He said not unless ritual manipulation is involved; some folk practices may work for pharmacological reasons without making modern medicine magic. | He says medicine is not magic merely because it changes natural conditions or fights disease. A practice would become magic only if it used ritual manipulation of forces, while herbs or folk remedies may work through ordinary pharmacological effects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=9455" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:35</a> | Could the placebo effect intersect with magic? | Yes. He said placebo can intersect with magical practices, but it is better understood as applied psychology. | He allows overlap because a magical ritual could trigger expectation, confidence, or psychosomatic response. Still, he treats placebo as psychology rather than evidence that the ritual has real magical causal power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=9647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:47</a> | Was the sacrificial system blood magic? | No. He said sacrifice is prescribed covenantal ritual, not stochastic magic meant to manipulate nature. | He says Israelite sacrifice is commanded covenant worship, not an attempt to force nature or spirits through technique. The blood has ritual and covenantal meaning within God's prescribed system, which makes it categorically different from magical manipulation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=9786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:06</a> | Is the Egyptian low chronology the majority view or the general consensus, and when did that consensus emerge? | He called it the grudging general consensus, saying it became essentially settled by the 1990s after the high chronology collapsed. | He describes the low chronology as the current general consensus, though not always enthusiastically held. In his account, the high chronology effectively collapsed by the 1990s, leaving the lower framework as the position scholars generally work from. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=10221" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:21</a> | With Passover coming up, could you discuss the historical context of the initial Passover in Exodus? | He placed it in the 19th Dynasty under Ramesses II, based on toponyms, Pharaoh's proximity to Goshen, and the Merneptah Stele. | He places the event around the 19th Dynasty, especially the reign of Ramesses II, because the Exodus route names fit that period and Pharaoh must be near Goshen for the night sequence in Exodus 12 and Numbers 33 to work. The Merneptah Stele's reference to Israel in Canaan by about 1217 BC narrows the window further, making the first half or middle of Ramesses II's reign his favored historical setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=10685" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:05</a> | Who is the angel of death? | He said the text is not fully clear, but he thinks it is the angel of the Lord and likely a theophany. | He says the identity is not fully explicit in the text, so certainty is limited. His own view is that the figure is the angel of the Lord, understood as a theophany, but he presents that as his judgment rather than an uncontested identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=10818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:18</a> | Why would an Israelite believe Moses about the Passover event? | He said the Israelites had already seen the earlier plagues, so Moses had demonstrated that his warnings should be trusted. | He says Moses had already demonstrated divine authority through the previous plagues: water turned to blood, frogs, flies, gnats, darkness, dead cattle, boils, and other judgments. After seeing those signs, Israelites had reason to trust Moses when he warned that the final plague would be worse and told them to prepare the Passover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=10893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:33</a> | What should I know about Leviticus to create a study guide? | He said Leviticus is about worshiping God God's way, especially through priestly ritual, holiness, cleanness, and sacrifice. | He says Leviticus is fundamentally about Levites, priesthood, ritual law, sacrifice, holiness, clean and unclean categories, and proper worship. His summary theme is that if Israel wants to worship God, it must worship in God's appointed way rather than inventing its own ritual terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=11100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:00</a> | What happened at Trinity Evangelical Divinity School? | He said TEDS was effectively disintegrating through financial mismanagement, bad decisions, and a takeover arrangement with Trinity Western University. | He says Trinity Evangelical Divinity School was ceasing to function as it had, describing the situation as institutional disintegration rather than a healthy transition. He attributes it to financial mismanagement and bad decision-making, and he frames the Trinity Western arrangement as a sign of broader seminary-sector failure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=11307" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:27</a> | Are most seminaries closing or struggling to stay open? | He said yes, largely because many are making similar decisions that reduce rigor and damage their long-term viability. | He says many seminaries are struggling because they make survival choices that weaken the very educational purpose they exist to serve. He connects declining rigor, financial pressure, and short-term institutional decisions with long-term collapse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=11382" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:42</a> | Will Candy's new workplace be another seminary domino? | He said Dallas Theological Seminary is not beyond recovery, but bad hires can be a warning sign of institutional decline. | He does not say Dallas Theological Seminary is doomed, but he treats questionable hiring and institutional choices as warning signs. In his framework, a seminary can still recover if it reverses bad decisions before they become structural decline. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=11528" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:08</a> | Are MDiv-lites like certificates or lighter course loads? | He said they are lighter course loads, often reducing the language requirements that make a traditional MDiv longer and harder. | He describes MDiv-lite programs as reduced versions of the traditional degree rather than mere certificates. The usual reduction, in his criticism, is lighter coursework and especially fewer biblical-language requirements, which lowers the academic burden of ministerial training. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=11642" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:02</a> | Does the name Kadesh Barnea being linked to Merneptah contradict Ramesses II as the Exodus Pharaoh? | No. He said there is enough chronological overlap between the wilderness period, Merneptah's campaign, and Israel's entry into Canaan. | He says the chronology is not contradictory because a Ramesses II Exodus still allows Israel to be in the wilderness and then in Canaan before Merneptah's campaign. The relevant names and events overlap within the late 13th-century framework rather than forcing a different Pharaoh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=11884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:04</a> | How do I discern the Holy Spirit's inspiration from my own subconscious, or God's calling from self-delusion? | He said God's calling is seen in providential circumstances and life direction, while self-generated inner messages should be treated cautiously. | He advises caution about treating internal impressions as direct divine speech. In his answer, God's calling is better discerned through providence, circumstances, gifts, responsibilities, and a life path that opens in concrete ways rather than through an isolated private feeling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=12350" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:50</a> | Which is worse, fundamentalism or progressive Christianity? | He said progressive Christianity is worse because he does not regard it as historic Christianity, while fundamentalism remains recognizably Christian despite excesses. | He criticizes both, but distinguishes them by their relation to historic Christianity. Fundamentalism may be excessive or anti-intellectual in his view, but he still regards it as recognizably Christian, whereas progressive Christianity has, in his judgment, departed from core Christian identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=12428" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:08</a> | What are your thoughts on the Cosmic Skeptic versus David Wood debate? | He had not watched it, so he declined to give a real assessment. | He explicitly declines to analyze the debate because he had not watched it. The answer is intentionally limited: without seeing the exchange, he will not offer a substantive judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=12498" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:18</a> | Did Kitchen account for Joseph being sold for 20 shekels in the Levant and not Egypt? | He said Kitchen used data from Egypt and Mesopotamia to normalize slave-price patterns, rather than isolating one region. | He says Kitchen's argument is comparative and regional rather than narrowly limited to Egypt or the Levant alone. The point is to observe broader ancient Near Eastern slave-price trends across Egypt and Mesopotamia so Joseph's 20 shekels can be situated within a historical price pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=12744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:24</a> | Could some atheist critiques of Christians be the demarcation problem? | Yes. He said critiques that equate Christianity with young-earth creationism are a demarcation problem because that view does not define Christianity. | He says some atheist critiques misidentify the boundary of Christianity by treating young-earth creationism as if it were the defining Christian position. That is a demarcation problem because attacking a subset or fringe view does not automatically address historic Christianity as a whole. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=12985" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:25</a> | Do you have a Discord? | Yes. He said the Discord is a social community linked in the description, but not a place where he normally answers formal questions. | He says there is a Discord linked in the video description, but he frames it as a community and social space. He clarifies that formal questions should generally be asked in the livestream format rather than expecting detailed answers from him there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=13150" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:10</a> | What is your favorite honorific, and who accrued the most honorifics in ancient Near Eastern history? | He said Senenmut probably accumulated the most, and explained Egyptian honorifics as a kind of status resume for the afterlife. | He identifies Senenmut as probably the figure with the most accumulated honorifics, while explaining that Egyptian titles functioned as more than ornament. They recorded offices, honors, and social status in a way that could serve as an enduring resume, including for the afterlife. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=13475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:35</a> | Are Genesis 10:14-15 a gloss on the Philistines, and should translations use Hethites instead of Hittites? | He said Genesis 10 is ethnography, the Philistines reference need not be a gloss, and Heth should be translated as Hittites. | He says Genesis 10 is doing ethnographic work, so the Philistines reference does not have to be dismissed as a later gloss simply because of later Philistine prominence. On Heth, he favors Hittites as the translation because the biblical ethnonym is connected with that people group. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=13668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:48</a> | How would you answer the claim that Ezekiel 20:5-25 cannot reference Exodus-to-Deuteronomy events because Ezekiel lacked a fully redacted Pentateuch? | He called that a red herring, saying Ezekiel could draw on Israel's Exodus tradition even without a fully redacted Pentateuch. | He says the argument confuses access to tradition with possession of the final Pentateuch. Ezekiel could know and use Israel's Exodus, wilderness, and law traditions even if the Pentateuch had not yet reached its final redacted form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:23</a> | Could the recently found tomb of Thutmose II help determine whether he was Moses? | No. He said Thutmose II was a king, the tomb is badly damaged, and the date is far too early for Moses. | He rejects the identification on chronological and contextual grounds. Thutmose II is a Pharaoh from a much earlier period than the Exodus chronology he defends, and a damaged royal tomb cannot turn that king into Moses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14060" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:20</a> | What can be drawn from the discovery of Neo-Assyrian-style armor in northwest China? | He said it shows trade and technology transfer between China and the Neo-Assyrian world, not necessarily Assyrian ownership of the armor. | He treats the armor as evidence for contact, exchange, or diffusion of military technology across long distances. He cautions that Neo-Assyrian style does not require the armor to have been owned by Assyrians themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14137" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:37</a> | Was the Last Supper a seder? | He said yes, he thinks it was a first-century seder that anticipates the Passover. | He says the Last Supper was a seder in a first-century Jewish sense, while also emphasizing that it anticipates the coming Passover events. The answer is brief but affirmative, connecting the meal to Passover rather than treating it as an unrelated dinner. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14167" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:07</a> | Why didn't the chariots leave while waiting for the Israelites to cross the sea or after the pillar of fire stopped them? | He said they were under orders and would likely face punishment or execution if they refused to pursue the Israelites. | He says Egyptian chariot troops would not simply abandon pursuit because they were under royal military orders. Refusing to follow Pharaoh's command in that setting could bring severe punishment, so they stayed with the mission even after delay and danger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:44</a> | Was there an Egyptian equivalent to Yahweh or Elohim, and how would Egyptians have understood Yahweh? | He said not really; Egyptian gods occupied smaller, local or pantheon roles, while Yahweh's exclusive and universal claims did not fit Egyptian metaphysics. | He says Egyptian religion had many gods with localized roles, domains, and mythic relationships, but no true equivalent to Yahweh's exclusive, universal sovereignty. Egyptians could categorize Yahweh as a foreign deity, but Yahweh's claim to be the only God over all reality would not fit their normal theological framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:58</a> | If someone lives virtuously without Christ, why believe in Christianity? | He said all have sinned, virtue cannot save, and salvation depends on one's relationship with Christ. | He answers from Christian soteriology: moral effort is not the basis of salvation because everyone has sinned. Christianity is necessary not because virtue is worthless, but because reconciliation with God depends on Christ rather than on being comparatively virtuous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=14745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:45</a> | In Revelation 9:11, who are Abaddon and Apollyon? | He said Abaddon/Apollyon is a fallen angelic prisoner, the worst figure in the abyss, not a guard of it. | He identifies Abaddon or Apollyon as a destructive fallen angelic figure associated with the abyss. In his reading, this being is not a jailer guarding the abyss but the worst imprisoned figure released from it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=15215" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:13:35</a> | Would you be willing to write a commentary on Revelation? | Yes, but he said he would only take on that much work with a publishing contract in hand. | He says he would be willing in principle, but only if a publisher contracted the project. A Revelation commentary would require substantial labor, so he would not take it on speculatively without formal support and a clear publication path. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=15354" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:15:54</a> | Why is G. K. Beale's Revelation commentary so well known? | He said Beale's strongly Christocentric method appeals to New Testament scholars, though he personally prefers starting with Old Testament context. | He says Beale is influential partly because his Christocentric approach resonates with New Testament scholarship. He respects the importance of the work but says his own method would begin more heavily with Old Testament context before moving to Christological interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=15528" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:48</a> | Could any demons have overthrown Satan, or was the demonic hierarchy set when the fall happened? | He said the hierarchy was already established at creation and carried over among fallen beings according to their power and roles. | He says spiritual hierarchy predates the fall because beings were created with different powers and offices. When some fell, that order carried over into the fallen realm, so he does not imagine demons freely reorganizing the hierarchy by overthrowing Satan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=15615" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20:15</a> | Does Christocentric theology weaken interfaith dialogue for Christians? | He said the main problem is not dialogue but exegesis and interpretive method, because it can skip the Old Testament's original context. | He says the issue is not primarily whether Christians can have interfaith dialogue, but whether their exegesis jumps too quickly to Christ without first understanding the Old Testament text in context. His concern is methodological: Christocentric readings can become weak if they bypass the earlier textual and historical setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=15828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:23:48</a> | What do you think of the claim that Noah assaulted his sons and that this is the nakedness they were trying to cover? | He rejected it, saying the euphemism concerns Noah's wife and incest, not Noah assaulting his sons. | He rejects the claim as the wrong reading of the nakedness language. In his interpretation, the euphemism concerns Noah's wife and an incestuous violation, not Noah assaulting his sons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=16142" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:29:02</a> | How can I avoid being cast into outer darkness like the man without a garment in Matthew 22:1-14? | He said to accept Christ, live in repentance and faith, and not treat Christianity as nominal fire insurance. | He says the warning is not answered by nominal affiliation or treating Christianity as insurance against judgment. The proper response is repentance, faith in Christ, and a life that actually reflects belonging to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/7-_-pSlLCgw?t=16732" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:38:52</a> | Did Jews of old believe in a personal Satan? | He said early views varied; Satan began more as an adversary role, later identified with the fallen angelic enemy of humanity. | He says earlier Jewish texts can use satan language for an adversarial role rather than the fully developed personal enemy familiar from later theology. Over time, that role becomes identified with a fallen angelic figure who opposes God and humanity. |
